perturbation.original_text: 7.2 Insurance. The Dolphins shall, at its own expense, maintain in effect throughout the term of this Agreement, comprehensive general liability insurance policies with carriers of recognized standing, with limits of liability of at least One Million Dollars ($1,000,000), governing any and all property damage and person injury (including death) arising out of activities covered by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.2 Insurance. The Dolphins shall maintain general liability insurance policies.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'at its own expense', 'in effect throughout the term of this Agreement', 'comprehensive', 'with carriers of recognized standing', and 'with limits of liability of at least One Million Dollars ($1,000,000), governing any and all property damage and person injury (including death) arising out of activities covered by this Agreement' significantly weakens the insurance requirement.  It creates a legal contradiction with standard business practices and potentially violates implied duties of good faith and fair dealing that require adequate insurance coverage.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing under Florida Law
perturbation.law_citation: Chikovsky v. Bank of America, N.A., 157 So.3d 296 (Fla. 3d DCA 2015)
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing by removing the specificity of insurance coverage, potentially leaving the other party inadequately protected. Florida law recognizes that every contract contains an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, meaning parties must act honestly and reasonably. Vague insurance requirements can be interpreted as a lack of good faith. Without defining minimum coverage, there's a legal risk of breach of contract if the actual insurance is insufficient.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandated specific insurance requirements (comprehensive, with recognized carriers, minimum liability) to cover potential damages and injuries. The changed text eliminates all these specifics, weakening the protection for the other party. This omission creates a loophole where the insurance coverage could be inadequate, contradicting the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing as explained in the LLM's Law Explanation. While the provided scraped law snippet doesn't directly address insurance requirements, the Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation, and the contrast between the original and changed text support the conclusion that the change creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.3 Any and all public announcements or press releases by or on behalf of the other party regarding the Sponsorship Rights or the details of this Agreement shall be subject to the consent of the other party, and each party shall have the right to approve in advance the contents and timing thereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Dolphins acknowledge that Hydron, as a publicly held company, has disclosure obligations pursuant to the federal securities laws. Hydron agrees to take the comments of the Dolphins into account in preparing and disseminating such disclosure, but notwithstanding comments from the Dolphins, Hydron shall make such disclosure as may be required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.3 Any and all public announcements or press releases by or on behalf of the other party regarding the Sponsorship Rights or the details of this Agreement shall be subject to the consent of the other party, and each party shall have the right to approve in advance the contents and timing thereof. Hydron agrees to take the comments of the Dolphins into account in preparing and disseminating such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the text 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Dolphins acknowledge that Hydron, as a publicly held company, has disclosure obligations pursuant to the federal securities laws.  ...Hydron shall make such disclosure as may be required by law.' creates a legal risk for Hydron. By removing this acknowledgment, the contract implies Hydron must obtain the Dolphins' consent before any disclosure, potentially conflicting with the company's legal obligations under securities laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which requires publicly traded companies to disclose material information to investors. By omitting the clause acknowledging Hydron's obligations under federal securities laws, the contract suggests Hydron needs the Dolphins' consent for all disclosures. This conflicts with Hydron's mandatory duty to disclose material information irrespective of the Dolphins' approval, creating a legal risk of securities law violations.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause acknowledging Hydron's disclosure obligations under federal securities laws creates a contradiction. The original text explicitly states Hydron's obligation to make disclosures as required by law, even with comments from the Dolphins. By removing this, the changed text implies that Hydron needs the Dolphins' consent for all disclosures. This is contrary to the obligations imposed on publicly traded companies which require them to disclose material information irrespective of any other party's approval, as suggested by the Law Explanation and the Perturbation Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (15 USC 78j) indicates that manipulative or deceptive devices are unlawful, which could be interpreted as failing to disclose material information as required.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 In the event that the consideration is not paid by Hydron on or before the applicable payment due date, said failure to pay shall be considered a material breach by Hydron, and the Dolphins may elect to charge Hydron a late fee of 1.5% per month of the payment then due and owing until it is paid in full. The Dolphins agree to provide written notice to Hydron of the failure to receive any payment, and Hydron shall have a five (5) business day period following delivery of written notice in which to cure the payment default before the Dolphins may elect to terminate this Agreement and pursue applicable remedies. It is agreed by Hydron that any such election of remedies does not waive any other remedies for breach of contract available to the Dolphins.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 In the event that the consideration is not paid by Hydron on or before the applicable payment due date, said failure to pay shall be considered a material breach by Hydron, and the Dolphins may elect to charge Hydron a late fee of 1.5% per month of the payment then due and owing until it is paid in full. It is agreed by Hydron that any such election of remedies does not waive any other remedies for breach of contract available to the Dolphins.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'The Dolphins agree to provide written notice to Hydron of the failure to receive any payment, and Hydron shall have a five (5) business day period following delivery of written notice in which to cure the payment default before the Dolphins may elect to terminate this Agreement and pursue applicable remedies' omits a critical protection for Hydron.  Without a notice and cure period, the Dolphins could immediately pursue remedies, including termination, for a late payment, even if the delay is minor or easily rectified. This omission could be seen as violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Florida law, as it creates an unreasonable advantage for the Dolphins.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing under Florida Law
perturbation.law_citation: Burger King Corp. v. Weaver, 169 F.3d 1310 (11th Cir. 1999)
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Florida law requires parties to a contract to act honestly and reasonably. By removing the notice and cure period, the contract allows the Dolphins to potentially terminate the agreement without giving Hydron a chance to rectify a late payment. This could be seen as an unfair or unreasonable exercise of contractual power, violating the implied covenant of good faith.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the notice and cure period in section 3.2 directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The original text provided Hydron a five-day window to rectify payment defaults after written notice, a standard protection in contracts. By removing this, the Dolphins could immediately pursue termination for late payments, violating the implied covenant of good faith. This creates an unreasonable advantage and fails to ensure fair dealing, as the omitted text removes a legally mandated consumer protection by potentially allowing termination without any chance for the other party to cure the breach.
